questions.question: Which selector type has the highest specificity?
questions.explanation: ID selectors have a specificity score of 0,1,0,0, making them higher than class (0,0,1,0) and element (0,0,0,1) selectors.

questions.question: What is the specificity score of the selector: .nav > li:hover?
questions.explanation: .nav is 1 class (0,0,1,0), :hover is 1 pseudo-class (0,0,1,0), and li is 1 element (0,0,0,1). Total: 0,0,2,1.

questions.question: What is the difference between :is() and :where() pseudo-classes?
options: They are identical in all ways
:is() takes the specificity of its most specific argument; :where() always has 0 specificity
:where() takes the specificity of its most specific argument; :is() always has 0 specificity
:is() only works in modern browsers; :where() is widely supported
questions.answer: :is() takes the specificity of its most specific argument; :where() always has 0 specificity
questions.explanation: :where() is useful for base/reset styles because its 0 specificity makes it easy for component-level styles to override it.

questions.question: What does the :has() selector enable that wasn't possible in CSS before?
options: Selecting the parent element based on its children
questions.answer: Selecting the parent element based on its children
questions.explanation: :has() is the long-awaited 'parent selector' — it allows styling an element based on what it contains, e.g., .card:has(img) to style cards that contain images.

questions.question: Which CSS declaration wins when two rules have the same specificity?
options: The first rule in the stylesheet
The last rule in the stylesheet
questions.answer: The last rule in the stylesheet
questions.explanation: When specificity is equal, the cascade resolves the conflict by source order — the rule that appears later in the stylesheet wins.
